refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications for all captured elements, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface topology data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the operational area.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate captured terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.5: Provide query and retrieval access to worksite model data for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query and update APIs.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel-grid occupancy model with semantic labels; support incremental updates and spatial queries; export to planning-compatible formats (costmaps, navigation graphs) on demand.

DP4: Establish a feature observation pipeline: raw sensor streams → feature extraction (detection/segmentation) → temporal fusion (tracking, consistency checks) → persistent model store; decouple acquisition from representation via a message-based event stream.

DP5: Represent worksite as a hybrid model: dense geometric mesh for navigation/collision-checking + sparse semantic annotations (safety zones, equipment placement constraints) linked via spatial indexing; support both continuous and discrete feature queries.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a feature observation pipeline: raw sensor streams → feature extraction (detection/segmentation) → temporal fusion (tracking, consistency checks) → persistent model store; decouple acquisition from representation via a message-based event stream.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality and feature extraction logic (mitigated by abstracting sensor input as normalized streams)
Dependency on temporal fusion strategy (e.g., Kalman filtering vs. graph-based SLAM) affecting model consistency; recommend parameterizing fusion policy
Coupling between feature extraction algorithms and downstream planning/execution consumers if schema changes; mitigated by versioning and schema evolution contracts
Message-based decoupling reduces direct caller-callee coupling but introduces eventual-consistency assumptions; acceptable given worksite dynamics
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves loose coupling through pipeline stages and event-driven architecture. Sensor acquisition is decoupled from model representation. Feature extraction is isolated as a reusable stage. Temporal fusion is a distinct concern. Persistent store is abstracted. This supports independent evolution of sensing hardware, algorithms, and downstream consumers.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite features are observable and extractable from available sensor modalities (vision, range, inertial) with acceptable latency and accuracy for planning/execution.
ASSUMPTION: Temporal fusion (tracking, consistency) can resolve conflicting or noisy observations within acceptable time windows (e.g., <5 seconds for dynamic obstacles).
ASSUMPTION: Downstream planning/execution functions can tolerate eventual consistency (model updates lag real-time sensor data by fusion window duration).
ASSUMPTION: Feature schema is stable or versioned; breaking schema changes do not occur frequently.
RISK: If sensor modalities are heterogeneous or unreliable (e.g., GPS-denied, low-light), feature extraction may fail or degrade; recommend fallback to manual annotation or reduced-fidelity model.

RISK: If worksite changes rapidly (e.g., dynamic obstacles, temporary barriers), temporal fusion window may be insufficient; recommend adaptive fusion or real-time streaming for high-priority features.
RISK: Message-based decoupling introduces latency and potential message loss; recommend idempotency and replay semantics for critical updates.
RISK: Persistent model store becomes a bottleneck if query load is high; recommend caching and spatial indexing strategies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Sensor abstraction layer (normalize heterogeneous inputs to feature observation streams), (2) Feature extraction stage (detection, segmentation, classification—parameterized by worksite safety/operational policies), (3) Temporal fusion stage (tracking, consistency, conflict resolution—with configurable fusion window and fallback strategies), (4) Model persistence (versioned schema, spatial indexing, query optimization), (5) Consumer interface (query API, subscription/notification for model updates). Add explicit acceptance criteria: model latency <T, feature detection recall >R%, schema versioning support, fallback to manual annotation if extraction confidence <C%. Clarify ownership boundaries (IM layer) for each stage to enable independent team assignment and testing.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized feature observation pipeline encompassing sensor stream ingestion, feature extraction (detection/segmentation), temporal fusion (tracking, consistency checks), and persistent model store management. Acts as the authoritative perception and state representation layer for the autonomous system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Raw sensor streams (camera, lidar, radar, IMU) ingested from Vehicle; carries unprocessed observations for feature extraction pipeline

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Processed feature observations, tracked entities, temporal fusion results, and persistent model state exported to Vehicle and Teleoperation for decision-making and visualization

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Critical safety-relevant observations (e.g., collision-imminent detections, anomalous state transitions) escalated to Safety Services for intervention

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional remote telemetry streaming and model synchronization for distributed or cloud-based feature store redundancy

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE is the perception/intelligence layer; feature observation pipeline is its core responsibility. Raw sensor ingestion (Telemetry in), feature extraction, temporal fusion, and model persistence are all native to NGHE's domain. Minor diffusion: safety-critical alerts must couple to Safety Services (SafetyAlert out), and processed features must couple to Vehicle/Teleoperation (Telemetry out), but these are expected cross-layer dependencies.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the physical platform and actuation layer, not perception. Placing the feature pipeline here would scatter perception logic across the vehicle control boundary, creating tight coupling between sensing and actuation. Feature extraction, temporal fusion, and model persistence belong upstream of Vehicle, not within it.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer for remote control and monitoring. The feature observation pipeline is a real-time autonomous perception system; placing it here conflates perception with operator interaction, creating architectural inversion. Pipeline must feed Teleoperation, not reside within it.

Very poor fit. Safety Services is a reactive safety enforcement layer (monitoring, intervention, alerts). The feature observation pipeline is a proactive perception and state-tracking system. While Safety Services consumes safety-critical observations from the pipeline, it should not own the pipeline itself; doing so would blur the separation between perception and safety policy.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The feature observation pipeline is the foundational perception and state representation layer of an autonomous system. It logically belongs in the NGHE (Next-Generation Heterogeneous Execution) Solution module, which is responsible for intelligent perception, feature extraction, temporal reasoning, and persistent model management. This placement ensures cohesion: all components of the pipeline (raw ingestion, feature extraction, temporal fusion, model store) are tightly coupled by data flow and share a common purpose (building and maintaining a consistent world model). External coupling is minimal and well-defined: Telemetry interfaces carry sensor input and processed output; SafetyAlert carries safety-critical escalations; Network Link supports optional distributed persistence. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are downstream consumers of the pipeline's outputs, not owners.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not yet architecturally isolated from Vehicle, the pipeline may diffuse into vehicle control logic, creating circular dependencies. Mitigation: Enforce strict interface boundaries (Telemetry in/out) and ensure NGHE is a separate deployment unit.

Risk: Temporal fusion and tracking require low-latency, deterministic execution. If NGHE Solution is implemented as a distributed or cloud-based service, network latency may violate real-time constraints. Mitigation: Ensure the core pipeline (feature extraction, fusion, model store) runs on-vehicle or in a co-located edge compute layer with bounded latency guarantees.
Risk: Persistent model store may grow unbounded if not managed. Mitigation: Define retention policies and archival strategies within NGHE Solution's scope.

Risk: Safety Services may need to override or reset the model state in failure scenarios. Mitigation: Define explicit safety-driven reset interfaces between Safety Services and NGHE Solution's model store.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) alignment with worksite safety plan and operational policies. NGHE Solution's IM boundary explicitly owns persistent model store and exports processed features via Telemetry interface to downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation). Traceability chain is clear: raw sensor ingestion → feature extraction → temporal fusion → model persistence → export to planning/execution.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated as the authoritative perception and state representation layer. Ownership is singular and well-scoped. Downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are clearly identified as consumers, not owners. Interface contracts (Telemetry in/out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) are explicit and directional. No ambiguity regarding which module is responsible for model generation, consistency, or persistence.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (feature observation pipeline: raw sensor streams → feature extraction → temporal fusion → persistent model store; message-based event stream decoupling) is a sound and complete solution to FR. The pipeline architecture directly addresses the functional requirement: (1) raw sensor capture is ingested, (2) features are extracted and segmented, (3) temporal consistency is maintained via tracking and fusion, (4) results are persisted in a machine-readable model store. Message-based decoupling between acquisition and representation ensures loose coupling and scalability. DP is technology-agnostic and logically sound; it does not prescribe implementation details prematurely.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Temporal fusion and tracking require low-latency, deterministic execution. If NGHE Solution is implemented as a distributed or cloud-based service with unbounded network latency, real-time constraints may be violated. Mitigation: Enforce on-vehicle or co-located edge compute deployment with bounded latency SLAs (e.g., <100ms end-to-end for safety-critical features). Define latency acceptance criteria in IM specification.

Risk: Persistent model store may grow unbounded if retention policies are not enforced. Diffusion risk: if model lifecycle management (archival, pruning, garbage collection) is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution, it may diffuse to downstream consumers or be neglected. Mitigation: Define and enforce retention policies, archival strategies, and storage quotas within NGHE Solution's IM scope. Document model lifecycle SLAs.

Risk: Safety Services may need to override, reset, or invalidate model state in failure scenarios (e.g., sensor failure, anomaly detection). If this capability is not explicitly designed into NGHE Solution's interfaces, Safety Services may attempt to directly manipulate the model store, creating architectural inversion. Mitigation: Define explicit safety-driven reset/override interfaces (e.g., 'ModelStateReset' command) between Safety Services and NGHE Solution. Ensure NGHE Solution validates and logs all state mutations.

Risk: Feature extraction and segmentation algorithms may be implemented as separate microservices or containers. If these are not tightly coupled within NGHE Solution's deployment boundary, data serialization/deserialization overhead and network latency may degrade real-time performance. Mitigation: Ensure feature extraction, fusion, and model store are co-located in a single deployment unit or tightly orchestrated container group with shared memory or high-bandwidth IPC.

Risk: If NGHE Solution is not architecturally isolated from Vehicle module (e.g., if sensor drivers or low-level telemetry processing are embedded in Vehicle), the pipeline may diffuse into vehicle control logic, creating circular dependencies and tight coupling. Mitigation: Enforce strict module boundaries. Ensure Vehicle exposes only a clean Telemetry interface; all perception logic must reside in NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for NGHE Solution is justified. The feature observation pipeline is the core responsibility of the perception/intelligence layer. All primary components (raw ingestion, feature extraction, temporal fusion, model persistence) are cohesive and belong together. External coupling is minimal and well-defined: Telemetry in/out interfaces are clean data contracts; SafetyAlert out is a justified cross-layer dependency (safety escalation); Network Link inout is optional and supports distributed redundancy without compromising core functionality. Minor diffusion (0.08) reflects expected cross-layer dependencies, not architectural defects. Recommended containment hypothesis is sound: NGHE Solution should be a separate, independently deployable module with clear ownership of the entire perception pipeline. No alternative allocation (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is architecturally viable.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define latency and determinism requirements for the feature observation pipeline. Add acceptance criteria: 'Feature extraction and temporal fusion must complete within <100ms (or site-specific SLA) from raw sensor ingestion to model update.' Ensure NGHE Solution deployment architecture (on-vehicle, edge compute, or cloud) satisfies these constraints.

Revision 2: Define model lifecycle management policies within NGHE Solution's IM specification. Include: (1) retention period for raw observations, (2) archival strategy for historical models, (3) storage quota and overflow handling, (4) garbage collection triggers. Document these as part of the persistent model store interface contract.

Revision 3: Add explicit safety-driven reset/override interface between Safety Services and NGHE Solution. Define a 'ModelStateReset' or 'ModelInvalidation' command that allows Safety Services to trigger model state resets in failure scenarios. Ensure NGHE Solution logs and validates all state mutations for auditability.

Revision 4: Enforce strict module boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle. Ensure Vehicle exposes only a clean, unidirectional Telemetry interface (raw sensor streams in, processed features out). Prohibit Vehicle from implementing feature extraction, fusion, or model persistence logic. Document this boundary in the architecture specification.

Revision 5: Specify co-location and deployment constraints for NGHE Solution components. If feature extraction, temporal fusion, and model store are implemented as separate services, define IPC mechanism (shared memory, high-bandwidth network, or container orchestration) to minimize latency and ensure real-time performance. Document deployment topology in IM specification.

Revision 6: Add traceability from acceptance criteria to IM interfaces. Explicitly map: (1) 'worksite model/map generation' → persistent model store (IM responsibility), (2) 'availability to planning/execution functions' → Telemetry out interface (IM export), (3) 'alignment with worksite safety plan' → SafetyAlert out interface (IM escalation). Include verification method for each mapping.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR4 is well-formed, DP4 is sound, and NGHE Solution IM allocation is architecturally justified (containment 0.92, all gates pass). However, the recommended_revisions identify critical safety-driven and real-time constraints that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure verifiability and risk containment. Specifically: (1) latency/determinism requirements for feature observation pipeline demand sub-FR decomposition to enforce on-vehicle/edge deployment and bounded SLAs; (2) model lifecycle management (retention, archival, garbage collection) is a distinct functional concern requiring explicit ownership and interface definition; (3) safety-driven reset/override capability is a cross-layer safety requirement that must be decomposed as a separate FR to prevent architectural inversion; (4) strict module boundary enforcement between NGHE Solution and Vehicle requires explicit sub-FR for telemetry interface isolation. These are not minor implementation details—they are functional requirements that directly impact safety criticality (high) and real-time performance. Decomposing them now ensures they are explicitly allocated, verified, and traced through to IM. Proceeding to next level without decomposition risks diffusion of safety and latency concerns into downstream modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Ensure feature observation pipeline executes within site-specific latency bounds (e.g., <100ms end-to-end from raw sensor ingestion to model update) to satisfy real-time safety constraints.
FR4.2: Manage persistent model store lifecycle (retention, archival, garbage collection, storage quota enforcement) to prevent unbounded growth and ensure deterministic performance.
FR4.3: Provide explicit safety-driven model state reset/override interface to allow Safety Services to invalidate or reset model state in failure scenarios without direct manipulation of model store.

FR4.4: Enforce strict architectural isolation between NGHE Solution perception pipeline and Vehicle telemetry layer to prevent diffusion of feature extraction, fusion, or model persistence logic into vehicle control domain.

revise.why: FR4 statement is functionally correct but under-specifies critical safety and real-time constraints. Revise FR4 to explicitly include latency, determinism, and safety-driven reset requirements as part of the functional requirement, not as implementation details. Proposed revision: 'FR4: Capture, represent, and manage worksite spatial features in a machine-readable, real-time model that satisfies latency bounds, enforces model lifecycle policies, and provides safety-driven reset capabilities to support planning, execution, and safety services.' This revision elevates safety and real-time concerns to the functional level, ensuring they are decomposed and verified as explicit sub-FRs rather than buried in IM specifications.

assumptions: NGHE Solution will be deployed on-vehicle or at edge compute with bounded network latency (<100ms assumed; site-specific SLA to be confirmed).
Safety Services has authority to trigger model state resets in failure scenarios; this is a legitimate cross-layer safety dependency.
Vehicle module will expose only a clean, unidirectional Telemetry interface; no feature extraction or fusion logic will be embedded in Vehicle.
Persistent model store is a single, authoritative source of truth for worksite spatial features; no distributed or replicated model stores will be created without explicit architectural review.

Feature observation pipeline (raw ingestion, extraction, fusion, persistence) will be implemented as a cohesive, independently deployable module within NGHE Solution; no diffusion to downstream consumers.
open_risks: Risk: Latency SLA for feature observation pipeline is not yet quantified. Site-specific constraints (e.g., worksite size, sensor density, fusion complexity) may require <100ms or may tolerate >500ms. Mitigation: Conduct latency analysis and define acceptance criteria in FR4.1 before IM specification.

Risk: Model lifecycle management policies (retention period, archival strategy, storage quota) are not yet defined. Unbounded growth could degrade performance or exhaust storage. Mitigation: Define and document lifecycle policies in FR4.2 specification; include acceptance criteria for storage quota enforcement.

Risk: Safety-driven reset/override interface may create race conditions or consistency violations if not carefully designed. Concurrent model updates and resets could corrupt state. Mitigation: Define explicit state machine and locking semantics for FR4.3; ensure NGHE Solution validates and logs all state mutations.

Risk: Strict module boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle may be difficult to enforce if Vehicle module is legacy or tightly coupled to perception logic. Mitigation: Conduct architectural audit of Vehicle module; refactor if necessary to expose clean Telemetry interface.

Risk: Co-location and deployment constraints for NGHE Solution components may conflict with cloud-native or microservices architecture patterns. Mitigation: Define deployment topology and IPC mechanism in IM specification; validate against latency and determinism requirements.
evidence_needed: Latency analysis: Measure end-to-end latency from raw sensor ingestion to model update under nominal and worst-case conditions. Confirm <100ms (or site-specific SLA) is achievable with proposed DP4 and IM allocation.

Model lifecycle requirements: Conduct stakeholder interviews with Safety Services, Planning, and Execution modules to define retention period, archival strategy, and storage quota. Document as acceptance criteria for FR4.2.
Safety-driven reset interface specification: Define state machine, command semantics, and logging requirements for model state reset/override. Validate against safety plan and operational policies.
Vehicle module audit: Review Vehicle module architecture to confirm Telemetry interface is clean and unidirectional. Identify and refactor any embedded feature extraction or fusion logic.

Deployment topology validation: Confirm proposed deployment architecture (on-vehicle, edge compute, or cloud) satisfies latency and determinism requirements. Measure IPC latency if components are distributed.
Traceability matrix: Map FR4 and sub-FRs (FR4.1–FR4.4) to acceptance criteria, DP4, NGHE Solution IM interfaces, and verification methods. Ensure no gaps or ambiguities.
